documentTitle: Asymmetric Encryption: One Key Locks, Another Unlocks
lede: Asymmetric (public-key) encryption solves an old problem in cryptography: how can two parties exchange a secret without ever meeting to agree on one shared key? Instead of a single key for everyone, each party holds a pair of keys — one that can be handed out to anyone, and one that must stay secret forever. Try both flows below: sending a secret message, and attaching a digital signature — notice how the two keys get used in exactly opposite directions.

keyring.holderPublicShared: Held by Bob & anyone who's obtained it (including Alice)
keyring.holderPrivate: Held only by Bob, never shared

tamperDemo.computedHashLabel: Hash of the message Alice received
tamperDemo.signatureHashLabel: Hash recovered from the signature (using Bob's public key)

legend.title: Quick reference: which flow uses which key
legend.sub: General rule: the direction each key is used in flips between these two flows.

rows.startAction: Lock the message → receiver's PUBLIC key
rows.endAction: Unlock the message → receiver's PRIVATE key
rows.example: HTTPS session key exchange, encrypted email (PGP/GPG)
Software code signing, SSL/TLS certificates, blockchain transactions

footer: INTERACTIVE DIAGRAM — ASYMMETRIC ENCRYPTION: CONFIDENTIALITY & DIGITAL SIGNATURES

encryptSteps.title: Bob generates a key pair
encryptSteps.desc: Bob runs a one-way math function (a trapdoor function) that produces two keys at once: a public key and a private key. The two are mathematically linked, yet it's computationally infeasible to work out the private key from the public one alone.
fields: finding p and q from n alone

encryptSteps.title: Bob publishes his public key
encryptSteps.desc: This public key is SAFE to hand out anywhere — a website, an email, a key server. It doesn't matter if an attacker gets a copy too, because this key can only LOCK, never unlock.
fields: Bob's private key — never sent anywhere

encryptSteps.title: Alice obtains Bob's public key
encryptSteps.desc: Alice now has a copy of Bob's public key. It isn't secret — anyone can hold one. The security here doesn't depend on the public key staying hidden, only on the private key staying with Bob alone.

encryptSteps.title: Alice locks her message with Bob's public key
encryptSteps.desc: Alice writes a secret message and encrypts it using Bob's public key. Once locked, the message becomes scrambled ciphertext that can ONLY be opened by its matching counterpart — Bob's private key.

encryptSteps.title: The ciphertext travels over the open internet
encryptSteps.desc: This locked message can safely travel over an insecure channel. If it's intercepted, an attacker only sees random-looking data — without Bob's private key, this ciphertext can't be read at all.

encryptSteps.title: Bob unlocks the ciphertext with his private key
encryptSteps.desc: Only Bob's private key matches the public key that was used to lock it. Bob decrypts the ciphertext and recovers the original message, exactly as Alice wrote it.

encryptSteps.title: Takeaway: public locks, private unlocks
encryptSteps.desc: In this confidentiality flow, the direction is always the same: anyone can lock a message with the receiver's public key, but only the matching private key's owner can unlock it. That's what removes the need for exchanging a shared secret key upfront, unlike symmetric cryptography.

signSteps.title: Bob wants to prove a message truly came from him
signSteps.desc: Bob uses the same key pair as before. This time the goal isn't secrecy — it's proving authenticity: making sure the message really came from him and wasn't altered along the way.

signSteps.title: Bob creates a fingerprint (hash) of his message
signSteps.desc: A hash function turns any message — long or short — into a short, fixed-length string. The smallest change to the message produces a completely different hash.

signSteps.title: Bob encrypts that hash with his PRIVATE key
signSteps.desc: The result of encrypting the hash with the private key is what's called a digital signature. Since only Bob holds that private key, only Bob can produce a valid signature for this exact message.
fields: a digital signature, attached to the message

signSteps.title: The message + signature are sent to Alice
signSteps.desc: Both can travel over any channel, even an insecure one — this message doesn't need to be kept secret, anyone can read its contents. What matters is that its authenticity can be verified later.
fields: the message itself stays plainly readable

signSteps.title: Alice verifies the signature with Bob's public key
signSteps.desc: Alice recomputes the hash of the message she received, then uses Bob's public key to "open" the signature and recover the hash Bob originally created. If the two hashes MATCH, the message is proven authentic and unchanged.
fields: Alice's computed hash vs. the hash from the signature

signSteps.title: Takeaway: private signs, public verifies
signSteps.desc: In this authenticity flow, the direction flips compared to the confidentiality flow: the signer uses their OWN private key, and anyone can verify using the signer's PUBLIC key. Notice how the direction of key usage is the exact opposite of locking a secret message!
